justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance solved via Redis Lua script (compare-and-swap, functionally equivalent to SET NX), (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with explicit durability trade-off (Redis crash loses in-flight windows, RDB/AOF best-effort, PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE considered and rejected for latency), (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed with Timer Service polling sorted sets and cascading to highest-rated courier, (4) Hot key risk addressed implicitly via zone-scoped sharding and explicitly in scaling guidance (Redis Cluster sharding by zone in Phase 3), (5) Durability vs. latency trade-off is a primary trade-off entry with alternatives considered.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed Lua script design for atomic claim — correctly identifies that application-level locks don't work across instances and that atomicity must be at the Redis layer
Comprehensive fallback chain: timer fires → atomic EXPIRED transition → query top-rated courier → assign → confirmation push with 10s timeout → cascade to next courier
Durability trade-off explicitly quantified: 'at 500 concurrent windows, a failover loses at most ~500 orders' with reconciliation job for stuck BIDDING state
Strong pitfalls-to-avoid section that directly addresses failure modes: no database-first, no keyspace notifications for timers, no application-level locks
Scale math grounded in concrete numbers (500 windows × 30 couriers, 100K ops/sec Redis capacity) rather than vague 'it scales'

Hot key problem not called out as an explicit named risk — zone-based sharding is mentioned as a scaling path but the specific risk of one popular zone overwhelming a single Redis shard is not in the risks section
WebSocket reconnection storm identified as a high-severity risk with exponential backoff + jitter mitigation — shows operational maturity

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved via Redis Lua CAS script — explicitly recommends against WATCH/MULTI/EXEC and database-first approaches. (2) In-memory Redis state recommended with clear durability trade-off acknowledged (Redis crash loses in-flight bid windows, acceptable for 15-second ephemeral process). (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed via Timeout Scheduler reading from fallback sorted set. (4) Hot key / Redis scalability risks addressed — single-instance vertical scaling limit noted, sharding by zone mentioned for growth. (5) Durability vs. latency trade-off is a first-class trade-off entry (Redis vs Postgres with SELECT FOR UPDATE).

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed Redis Lua CAS design with concrete script pseudocode and explicit rejection of WATCH/MULTI/EXEC optimistic approach
Thorough state machine design (OPEN → CLAIMED | EXPIRED) with rigidity point explaining why additional states should be resisted
Correctly separates ephemeral state (Redis) from durable state (Postgres) with clear reasoning about when each is authoritative
Addresses clock skew risk by recommending Redis server TIME command rather than application clocks
Identifies courier network drop scenario and designs push notification + polling fallback — goes beyond the rubric requirements
Practical implementation guidance with phased approach and concrete file listing including the Lua scripts as the keystone

Hot key risk is addressed indirectly (single-instance scaling limit, shard by zone) but not framed explicitly as a 'hot key problem' — the concept is covered but could be more direct about per-key contention in popular zones
Sorted-set polling for timeouts over keyspace notifications shows depth of Redis operational knowledge
Bid slot cap per courier prevents winner-take-all effect — a second-order concern most architects miss

The design optimizes for correctness under concurrency first, latency second, and throughput third — the right priority order fo

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Atomic acceptance identified and solved via Redis Lua script (SETNX equivalent with conditional check-and-set), explicitly rejecting database-level locking. (2) In-memory state recommended with durability trade-off clearly acknowledged — trade_offs section details Redis crash data loss risk and async persistence to durable store. (3) Fallback auto-assignment on timer expiry designed — highest-rated courier auto-assigned via the same atomic claim path. (4) Hot key risk addressed indirectly through Redis Cluster sharding mention and directly through the 'hot courier problem' risk entry. (5) Durability vs. latency trade-off is a first-class trade-off entry. Additionally, clock skew between server timer and mobile apps is addressed, and the database-first approach is explicitly rejected with latency justification.

observations.text: Excellent use of Redis Lua script for atomic claims rather than naive SETNX — the Lua script approach handles the conditional check-and-set atomically, which is more robust than bare SETNX
Timeout race condition explicitly addressed — both courier accept and timeout use the identical Lua script, guaranteeing exactly-one-winner semantics by design
Durability vs. latency trade-off thoroughly explored with concrete alternatives (PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE at 5-10x slower, Redlock as unnecessary complexity)
Clock skew mitigation is well-designed: server-authoritative timer, server timestamp in offer payload, 500ms grace period
Implementation guidance is unusually actionable — phased rollout, specific file structure, concrete pitfalls-to-avoid list

Hot key problem for Redis sharding (many bid windows landing on the same shard for a popular zone) is not explicitly called out as a risk — the 'hot courier' risk is related but distinct from hot key/shard pressure
Domain model with bounded contexts and context map adds clarity to ownership boundaries and integration patterns
